entry.title: Re: Outgoing Belgium einvoices Message/Business Level Res...
entry.content:
Why you only see “Acknowledged by Receiving Access Point”
In the Peppol framework, the status “Acknowledged by Receiving Access Point” means the invoice has been successfully handed over to the receiver’s access point. The next status “Accepted by Receiving Access Point” requires a Message Level Response (MLR) from the receiver’s system. Many receivers (customers, suppliers, even your own test company) do not send MLRs back — either because their access point is not configured to generate them, or because their business process does not require it. That’s why, even though you see the acknowledgment, you never see the “Accepted” status: the MLR is simply not being returned.

What your test showed
You submitted an invoice from Belgium → Luxembourg. In Luxembourg, you manually triggered “Submit” to send the acknowledgment. The acknowledgment was visible in Peppol Exchange, but the Belgian side did not update to “Accepted.” This confirms that the sending system only updates to “Accepted” when an MLR is received. If the receiver does not send one, the status stays at “Acknowledged.”

Solution / Workaround
No extra SAP configuration is missing — the system is behaving as designed. Acceptance status depends entirely on the receiver’s access point sending back an MLR. If your customers’ access points do not send MLRs, you will never see “Accepted.” For audit or monitoring purposes, you can treat “Acknowledged by Receiving Access Point” as the final status unless you know the receiver’s AP supports MLR. If you want to test MLR end‑to‑end, you need a receiver AP that is configured to generate and return the MLR. Your manual “Submit” in Luxembourg was not equivalent to a proper MLR response.

entry.title: Re: 🤖⚡ Meet Traceability Agent, my first custom Joule Agent for SAP Digital Manufacturing. ⚡🤖
entry.content:
@ManoelCosta , that's my concern: I think it's a beautiful demo but I'm not sure this use case can be technically adapted to really work safely in productive environments.

You are speaking about "principal propagation and other mechnisms" but at the moment that you use the MDO access with the database account that can read all data, you have no way to propagate user account or prevent safely that the AI agent won't get a data that it is not supposed to get.

Globally, that's a complain I do about most of the demo/PoC (most with AI): It's working in demo/test environment but can't be deployed in production due to missing framework/functionalities in the product itself to deal with the security and fine authorizations.

For the moment, I don't see any technical and safe way to use Joule with SAP DM customer data without directly including it in the SAP DM product by the product team ("infused" as done for other SAP products).

entry.title: Batch Management in advanced intercompany process
entry.content: I have a material for which i am using Advanced intercompany scenario. This material is batch managed in delivering plant and not batch managed in the transit plant. Now i have created an initial sales order with no batch in the receiving company and purchase order gets created automatically in the same company. In the delivering company the subsequent sales order gets created automatically. I create the outbound delivery with reference to the sales order from the receiving company with manually entering the batch. I post the goods issue against this outbound delivery. After posting goods issue the goods receipt against the purchase order (GRTS) is posted automatically successfully without batch. Now when the system tries to post the material document Post GI from Storage Location (GIFS) automatically an error gets created. The error text is as follows - "Check table XMCHA: entry does not exist". I don't want to activate batch management in the transit plant. (Restriction of advanced intercompany process are known) How can i overcome this issue? Is there any customizing that will help?

entry.title: Re: Route determination for normal inbound deliveries
entry.content:
1) Route Determination Table
You maintained “Determine Route for Inbound Deliveries” with a Supplier + Plant combination. However, inbound route determination also depends on shipping conditions and transportation zones. If those are missing or not aligned, the system cannot determine a route.

2) Prerequisites for Route Determination
Shipping condition must be set in the inbound delivery. Transportation zone must be maintained for the supplier’s address. Transportation group must be maintained in the material master. The route determination table uses these fields (shipping condition + transportation group + transportation zone + delivery type) to find the route.

3) Shipping Data
For the route to populate automatically, you must ensure that when creating the inbound delivery with reference to a Purchase Order (PO) or Stock Transport Order (STO):

  • The PO has a shipping tab with relevant shipping data.
  • If the shipping condition is missing in the PO, it will not flow to the delivery.

entry.title: Re: Output Preview PDF for G/L Accounts Line Items base o...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

In this case, I asked an internal chatbot for support. The following information sources were used:

Output Parameter Determination

S/4HANA Cloud Output Management - Purchase Order

Maintain Form Templates

Here's the response:

  1. Access the Output Parameter Determination App

    • Use the Output Parameter Determination app  to configure the output parameters for your PDF generation. This app allows you to define the output type, receiver, channel, and other settings necessary for generating the PDF.

  2. Select the Appropriate Output Type

    • In the Output Parameter Determination app, select the output type that corresponds to your requirement. For example, if you are generating a Statement of Account, you might need to define a custom output type.

  3. Configure the Receiver and Channel

    • Ensure that the receiver is set to the appropriate role, such as the employee or worker for whom the statement is being generated. Set the channel to ‘PRINT’ if you want a physical printout or ‘EMAIL’ if you want to send it electronically.

  4. Define the Form Template

    • Use the Maintain Form Templates app to define or customize the form template that will be used for the PDF output. You can create a new form or modify an existing one to include the necessary fields, such as GL accounts and Personnel Number.

  5. Preview and Test

    • Once the configuration is complete, generate a test PDF to ensure that all the information is correctly displayed. Use the preview function to check the layout and data.

Additional Tips

  • Use Adobe LiveCycle Designer: If you need to create or modify form templates, Adobe LiveCycle Designer is a useful tool for designing forms.

By following these steps, you should be able to generate and preview a PDF output for a Statement of Account based on a Personnel Number.

I hope this helps.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Question about best practices for clients in SAP document AI?
entry.content:
Hi all

The word CLIENT seems very familiar when you're used to work with SAP ECC or S/4 HANA, but in BTP we have other options to separate data and maybe we would create subaccounts for the same purpose as clients in the ERP system.

So I wonder what we can use clients in SAP Document AI for.

Lets pretend that I have 5 different SAP Document AI projects 

  • load customer PO to sale sorder UK
  • Load customer PO to sales order PL
  • Load vendor invoice 
  • Load WM inventory documents
  • Load MM inventory documents

Now, I would very much like to separate schemas and loaded documents etc. for these project so they are not just in the same big pile of documents. Could I make this separation by creating one client per project?

Best regards
Thomas Madsen Nielsen

entry.title: SAP Academic Community Conference Latin America & the Caribbean | Share your motivation!
entry.content:
Welcome to the ACC LAC 2026: Let’s Start Connecting!

The SAP Academic Community Conference Latin America & the Caribbean 2026 (ACC LAC 2026) is happening May 19–21, 2026, in São Leopoldo, Brazil and we are excited to welcome faculty members from across the region for three inspiring days of learning, exchange, and collaboration.

To help us build meaningful connections before we meet in person, we warmly invite all confirmed participants to introduce themselves to the community, - participation is completely optional -  but we would truly love to receive your post and learn more about you!

 

How to Participate

If you are attending ACC LAC 2026, please share a short introduction of you to eduardo.oliveira@sap.com with the information below: 

  • Your full name

  • Your institution

  • Your title/position

  • A brief paragraph on your motivation to participate in ACC LAC 2026

  • A photo (optional, but encouraged!)

  • Your LinkedIn profile link

 

“Why am I attending ACC LAC 2026?”

We will share this content in our Welcome Guide to help everyone see who will be attending and start creating connections, synergies, research collaborations, and networking opportunities in advance.

Let’s use this opportunity to strengthen our academic network across Latin America and the Caribbean and make this conference even more impactful.

We look forward to learning more about you and seeing you in São Leopoldo!

entry.title: Re: Planned Depreciation Asset with value equal for Decli...
entry.content:
Hello @ArogyaAra ,

Please go through below alternate solution.

TopicPro-Rata at Period Start (Recommended)At Start of Fiscal Year (Force 12 Periods)
Basic LogicSAP calculates depreciation only from the acquisition period (October 2025).SAP ignores actual start date and assumes asset existed from 01.01.2025.
How 2025 Is CalculatedAnnual depreciation = 25,000. Since asset exists only 3 months in 2025, system calculates 25,000 × 3/12 = 6,250.System calculates full 25% for entire year = 25,000, even though asset started in October.
2025 Monthly Posting6,250 is divided equally across Period 10, 11, 12 → 2,083.33 each. No posting in Jan–Sep.25,000 is divided across all 12 periods → 2,083.33 per month including Jan–Sep.
Impact on Financial Statements (2025)Expense reflects actual usage period (only Oct–Dec). More accurate P&L.Expense includes depreciation for months before asset was in use. P&L shows higher expense.
Net Book Value at End of 2025100,000 – 6,250 = 93,750100,000 – 25,000 = 75,000
2026 Depreciation Base25% × 93,750 = 23,437.5025% × 75,000 = 18,750
2026 Monthly Posting23,437.50 ÷ 12 = 1,953.13 per period (equal).18,750 ÷ 12 = 1,562.50 per period (equal).
Equal Amount Each Period?Yes — but only for periods where depreciation is allowed (Oct–Dec in 2025, full 12 in 2026 onward).Yes — full 12 periods every year regardless of acquisition date.
Compliance / Accounting AccuracyMatches IFRS and GAAP principle of matching expense to usage period.May violate matching principle unless company policy allows full-year convention.
RiskNo accounting risk. System behaves naturally.Risk of overstating depreciation in first year. Auditor may question logic.
When To UseStandard fixed asset accounting. Most companies use this.When company policy uses “full-year convention” regardless of acquisition month.
SAP Configuration NeededPeriod Control Method = 01 (Pro rata at period start). Depreciation to the day = OFF.Period Control Method = 06 (At start of fiscal year). Depreciation to the day = OFF.
Best Practice RecommendationStrongly recommended.Use only if officially required by accounting policy.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: New in SAP EAM with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Task Planning Management API
entry.content:
Task Planning Management in S/4HANA Asset Management - SAP Media Share

 

Hello EAM community!

I’m Michael Spiess, team architect and scrum master of the Wall/E team. I’m excited to share our team’s first S/4HANA delivery — and it’s a big one!

What’s new:

  • Task Planning Management API: Programmatic access to roughly 90% of Compatible Unit Management capabilities. 
  • New feature for the Process Maintenance Order app (W0017). You can now assign a compatible unit design to a maintenance order. 

Enjoy exploring the new API, and trying out the app enhancement, and stay tuned — more updates from the Wall/E team are on the way!

 

What is Task Planning Management?

Task Planning Management (TPM) is the successor of Compatible Unit Management.

The primary reason for this rebranding is to emphasize that the compatible unit functionality will be enhanced to serve not only the utility industry but also other sectors.

These enhancements are planned for future releases; in the current release, the new term is only applicable to the APIs. 

 

What is Compatible Unit Management?

Compatible Unit Management is an enterprise extension in SAP EAM for organizations that construct and maintain physical assets using standard, repeatable units of work. With compatible units (CUs), you define a reusable work package for an asset or asset component and enrich it with job-specific details, such as:

  • Job steps and planned duration
  • Crew and skill requirements
  • Special tools and materials
  • Reference documents
  • Accounting and cost assignments

CUs are modular by design and typically mirror the engineering structure (EBOM) of an asset. They’re often authored in CAD systems and then transferred to SAP EAM to bring the CU design to life — from resource allocation for execution through to financial settlement.

For example, one CU might define a utility pole, and another might define the installation of crossarms on that pole. Multiple CUs can be bundled into a CU design document to represent the entire project, for example to build a utility connection for a new construction site.  In a planning phase you may be interested to discuss design variants before you decide together with the customer for the best option. This is why you can assign multiple design documents to a construction measure document and release the selected design document from there. The construction measure can be cross-linked with a WBS element, a notification, or a master maintenance order.

 

How do Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management relate to each other?

The new Task Planning Management API creates and updates compatible unit master data and has full access to the two documents in Compatible Unit Management:

  • Compatible Unit Design
  • Construction Measure

This diagram shows how the new business objects of Task Planning Management - built with latest RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) technology -  get full access to master data and documents from databases of the Compatible Unit domain.

Figure 1: Relationship between Task Planning Management and Compatible Unit Management

 

 

End users can still use Compatible Unit Management in SAP GUI while developers can already use the new business objects to access Compatible Unit Management functionality from API version 1.0.0 onwards.

 

What are the benefits of Task Planning Management?

 

Shorter design cycles

The Task Planning Management API streamlines the conversion of an engineering bill of material (EBOM) into a task planning design document which represents the  service bill of material (SBOM). CAD developers can easily consume the TPM OData V4 API as a web-extension. This to offers engineers  a seamless app experience, enabling them to create a task planning design document on the fly while working on the engineering bill of material.  Once the engineering design document is released, the task planning design document is simultaneously released. This becomes a single, reusable source of truth that maintenance planners can embed directly in maintenance orders for resource planning, execution, maintenance and financial settlement.

The following diagram shows  in three detail levels how CAD objects are mapped to the corresponding business objects of Task Planning Management:

Figure 2: EBOM to SBOM transformation example with Task Planning Management API

Enhanced work order efficiency

Whether you assign a task planning element to a reusable design document or ad-hoc to a maintenance order, selecting the right operations for each part of the build becomes straightforward. Thanks to a modular structure and inheritance from the parent planning element, data entry is minimal. In the following example the user inputs two values ("install", "classic") to estimate time and costs for a classic pole installation:

Figure 3: classification system and multi-level value inheritance boosts work efficiency

More accurate job estimates

When Operation Account Assignment (OAA) is enabled, you can track actuals down to each task planning element. This granular visibility allows teams to fine-tune planned costs for specific planning elements, resulting in more accurate quotes and better cost predictability across projects.

 

How will Task Planning Management evolve in the future?

Let’s take a look ahead! Please keep in mind that these are my personal views. For the most reliable view of what’s coming, explore SAP Road Maps at https://roadmaps.sap.com/ and search for “EAM.”

One of the hottest topics I see is the integration of Task Planning Management with SAP S/4HANA Service Management. Having a task planning design for execution and maintenance is fine, but using this design document easily in a quotation process doubles the value of Task Planning Management!

Integration with SAP Project System (PS) is another win. With precise durations and work centers derived from the design, project managers can confidently schedule construction phases and identify the optimal slots in the overall project plan. Bringing in SAP Geographical Enablement Framework (GEF) would be equally powerful. It would allow maintenance planners to search for installed planning elements and designs on a map, accelerating decisions and improving asset insights.

To tie these capabilities together, SAP’s AI assistant, Joule, is the natural companion! With Task Planning Management skills, Joule lets users adjust designs on the fly and guide them straight to the screens where those changes impact quote values and project timelines.

Figure 4: Task Planning Management agent communication

 

How can I try out the API?

 

Prerequisites

  • Business function for Compatible Unit Management switched on
  • Initial configuration for Compatible Unit Management completed
  • Permission to new authorization objects for Task Planning Management (I_TPMTPE, I_TPMDSGN, I_TPMCM) granted
  • Access to transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT 

 

Overview about the process - from task planning element via design to the new app feature

Start exploring the Task Planning Management API by creating your first task planning element in a few steps! Once the first task planning element is created, valuated, and released, you can further enhance it to a multi-level task planning element to represent a classic utility pole, like the pole from figure 3. You can use the pole in a new design document to test your first task planning element. To test out all three business objects you can create a construction measure and release the design from there. Use the new feature "Assign CU-Design" in app W0017 "Process Maintenance Order" to verify the successful execution of the request.

Here are the required requests in an overview:

Step

API request

How many times

1.0

Create task planning element 

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.1

Create value assignment

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.2

Release task planning element

3x (pole, cross arm, insulator)

1.3

Create task planning element BOM item

2x (pole, cross arm)

2.0

Create task planning design document

1x

2.1

Create the value assignment for the utility pole

1x

3.0

Create construction measure and release design 

1x

 

Step 1.0: Create a task planning element in 7 steps 

Let us create the first task planning element for an 40 ft wooden utility pole:

1.  SAP Logon to the S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 system

2. Call transaction /IWFND/GW_CLIENT

3. Change the radio-button from GET to POST

4. Copy this line into the Request URI text box:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement

5. Copy these lines into the HTTP-Request body box and replace the values with your compatible unit configuration:

{
  "TaskPlanningElementCategory": "1 - stands for internal numbering",
  "BaseUnitofMeasure": "PC",
  "TaskPlanningElementDescription": "utility pole 40 ft wooden",
  "TskPlngElmntPlantForWorkCenter": "Plant for Work Center of TaskListGroup",
  "TaskListGroup": "should be unique for this Task Planning Element",
  "FunctionalLocationLabelName": "FuncLoc or Equipment"
}

6. Execute the request.

7. HTTP 201 returned. Success! You created your first task planning element in just 7 steps. Your screen should look like this:  

Figure 5: Task Planning Element created with SAP Gateway Client

Let's take a closer look into the HTTP response. Copy the task planning identifier 1539025 to your clipboard for the next request.

{
  "@odata.context" : "$metadata#TaskPlanningElement/$entity",
  "@odata.metadataEtag" : "W/\"20260212230942\"",
  "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539025",  //use for next request
  "TaskPlanningElementCategory" : "1",
  "TaskListGroup" : "POLE1"
...
}

 

Step 1.1: Create value assignment for task planning element

Define the allowed range of values for the characteristic CU_ACTION. Supply also characteristics with mandatory value assignments like in my example "CU_TYPE".

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/SAP__self.CreateValueAssignment{
  "_CharcValueComponent": [  {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "INSTALL"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "REMOVE"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue": "INSPEC"
    },    {
      "Characteristic": "CU_TYPE",
      "CharcValue": "classic"
    }  ] }

 

Step 1.2: Release task planning element

Release the task planning element:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/SAP__self.Release

Repeat steps 1.1 to 1.3 to create and release the planning elements for the cross arm and the insulator. In my example it is task planning element id 1539027 for the cross arm and 1539028 for the insulator.

 

Step 1.3: Create task planning element BOM item

To reflect the engineering structure of the utility pole, define the pole as the root and the two cross arm as the first children:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539025/_TskPlngElmntBOMItem
{
  "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate": 1,      //execution factor for operation processing
  "BillOfMaterialComponent": "1539027",     // cross arm
  "NmbrOfCompatibleUnitInstances" : "2"     // 2 cross arms for one pole
}

Now each cross arm get's two insulators beneath:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningelement/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningelement/0001/TaskPlanningElement/1539027/_TskPlngElmntBOMItem
{
  "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate": 1,        //execution factor for operation processing
  "BillOfMaterialComponent": "1539028",       //insulator
  "NmbrOfCompatibleUnitInstances" : "2"       //2 insulator for one cross arm
}

You can verify the result by displaying the compatible unit structure list in transaction "Display Compatible Unit":

Figure 6: How to access the structure list - this icon is only visible for multi-level CUsFigure 7: Structure list of my example

 

Step 2.0 Create task planning design document

Use this request to create a new design document where you assign the pole planning element under the mandatory root structure element. To assign a planning element in the design hierarchy you need to define the node rank of the new element on your own. Use sequential numbers here , starting with 1. To define the parent element, you can either use the self-given parent node rank, or you can use the task planning assignment UUID in case the parent element already exists in the database.

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TaskPlanningDesign
{
  "TaskPlanningDesignProfile": "0000000001", // use your own profile
  "TaskPlanningDesignType": "D",             // design single order use
  "MainWorkCenter": "EAM-ELEC",
  "TaskPlanningDesignDescription": "new connection – 50 classic poles",
  "_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign": 
[ {
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank": "0001"         // root structure element
    },
    {
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank": "0001",     // root structure element
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank": "0002",        // pole planning element - node rank
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier": "1539025",  // pole planning element -id  
      "MaintOrdOperationExecutionRate" : 50        // we need 50 poles for the design
    }  ]
}

In the response, you receive eight planning element assignments: One root structure element, one pole, two cross arms, and four insulators in total.

{  // design document header 
  "@odata.context" :        
  "$metadata#TaskPlanningDesign(_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign())/$entity",
  "@odata.metadataEtag" : "W/\"20260212230942\"",
  "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
  "TaskPlanningDesignNumber" : "201568",
  "TaskPlanningDesignDescription" : "new connection - 50 utility poles",
  …
  ], 
  "_TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign" : [  
    { // root structure element
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8022a0e",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank" : "1",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "0",
      "LastTskPlngElmntParentNodeRank" : "0",
      "SAP__Messages" : [  ]
    },
    { // task planning element: pole 
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8024a0e",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539025",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "0",
      "LastTskPlngElmntParentNodeRank" : "0",
      "TaskListGroupCounter" : "1",
      "TaskListGroup" : "POLE1",
      ...
   },
...
    {   // task planning element insulator
      "TaskPlanningDesignUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtUUID" : "42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b56a2b",
      "TaskPlanningElementIdentifier" : "1539028",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtDescription" : "insulator",
      "StructureElementDefaultType" : "",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtNodeRank" : "8",
      "TskPlngElmntAssgmtParNodeRank" : "6"
...
]

 

Step 2.1: Create value assignment for the utility pole

You now set the values for the mandatory value assignment of the pole like a user would do it manually in figure 3. In my example, this is characteristic "CU Action" and "CU Type". To address the pole in the design document you need to use the design UUID and the task planning assignment UUID of the pole from the previous response in the request URI. 

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TskPlngElmntAssgmtDesign/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b42a2b/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8485-619ba2b46a2b/SAP__self.CreateValueAssignment
{
  "_CharcValueComponent": [
    {
      "Characteristic" : "CU_ACTION",
      "CharcValue" : "INSTALL"
    },
    {
      "Characteristic" : "CU_TYPE",
      "CharcValue" : "POLE"
    }
  ]
}

 

Step 3.0 Create Construction Measure and release design

Create a construction measure:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure
{
  "ConstructionMeasureDescription": "New connection for SAP headquarter",
  "ConstructionMeasureType": "0002" //for internal numbering
}

Release the construction measure:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure/10841/SAP__self.Release

Update the design with the created construction measure (in my example it is 10841). Use here the PATCH-request:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_taskplanningdesign/srvd_a2x/sap/taskplanningdesign/0001/TaskPlanningDesign/42010aef-acab-1fe1-8482-b858d8020a0e
{  "ConstructionMeasure": "10841"   }

 Release the design from the construction measure. Use here again the POST-request:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_constructionmeasure/srvd_a2x/sap/constructionmeasure/0001/ConstructionMeasure/10841/SAP__self.SelectForRelease
{  "TaskPlanningDesignNumber": "201568"    }

 

Verify your result: Assign CU-Design in WebDynpro app "Process maintenance order"

Create a maintenance order with a order type which is enabled for Compatible Units. Choose the action "Assign CU Design" at the operation data tab of the maintenance order and select the CU design you released in the previous step. The operations for the "Install" CU-Action are determined and exploded into the operation list. You can still see the link between operation and CU at the order item level. This becomes important if you want to track costs on operational accounting level (OAA).

Figure 8: Assign CU-Design 201568 in a maintenance order

 

Further Links

  • Task Planning Management on help.sap.com here
  • App feature on help.sap.com here
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_TASKPLANNINGELEMENT_0001-v1
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_TASKPLANNINGDESIGN_0001-v1
  • https://api.sap.com/api/sap-s4-OP_CONSTRUCTIONMEASURE_0001-v1

entry.title: Re: Asset Partial Transfer ABUMN
entry.content:
Hello @nag_7589 ,

SAP is not wrong — it is protecting accounting consistency.

Because depreciation was already posted in January 2026, the system does not automatically restructure the year after your February transfer.

From 2027 onward, everything works because SAP starts a fresh annual calculation.

 

What Happened Why SAP Behaved This Way & How to Fix

Asset A acquired in 2025 and depreciated normallyCorrect — full year depreciation calculated as expected.
In Jan 2026, EUR 200 wrongly posted again to Asset AAsset value increased to 1,200 → SAP calculated January 2026 depreciation on full 1,200.
January 2026 depreciation run already executedOnce depreciation is posted for a fiscal year, SAP does not automatically rebuild the year plan.
In Feb 2026, partial transfer (ABUMN) moved 200 to Asset BTransfer happened AFTER depreciation run, so SAP does not fully recalculate 2026 plan.
Asset A still shows higher planned depreciation for 2026Because 2026 depreciation structure was already created before the correction.
Asset B shows no planned depreciation in 2026Since recalculation of depreciation year was not triggered, SAP starts clean calculation from next fiscal year (2027).
In 2027 both assets depreciate correctlyNo depreciation posted yet for 2027 → SAP recalculates normally from updated asset values.
Root CauseTiming issue: depreciation run executed before correction posting. SAP protects posted fiscal year integrity.
How to Fix 2026Run Depreciation Recalculation (AFAR) and then rerun depreciation. This rebuilds 2026 plan correctly for both assets.
Alternative FixReverse January 2026 depreciation, then rerun depreciation (more effort, usually not required if AFAR works).

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: SAP DM - Global variable memory consumption with diff...
entry.content:
Hi Ankit,

You can technically work around the 2 MB global variable limitation by storing large textual data inside a dummy Work Instruction, and then loading it on demand from the POD.
This approach works because WI content is retrieved dynamically and is not bound by the same in‑memory size constraints as global variables.

However, even though this can help in specific cases, I would not consider it an ideal or scalable solution.

For anything beyond small or static text blobs, the more robust and future‑proof approach is to rely on an external database or persistence layer (e.g., Data Store, HANA, external DB, microservice API, etc.) and only keep keys/references in the global variable.

This gives you:

  • No size limitation
  • Better data management
  • Easier maintainability
  • More predictable performance

So the WI workaround can help for very specific edge cases, but for real data volumes I strongly recommend an external storage solution rather than trying to expand what a global variable can hold.

In addition to the workaround with a dummy Work Instruction (which can work for static or semi-static textual payloads), another option is to offload the large data into SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: Re: Dear Experts,We are looking to exclude certain servic...
entry.content:
Hello @Khushboo_singhal ,

Below is the explnation for your issue.

 

What You Are ObservingWhy It Happens & How To Fix
You deactivated RAR relevancy in configuration for certain service typesDeactivating RAR relevancy at configuration level does NOT automatically remove RAR indicator from already defined provider contract items.
RAR relevancy indicator still appears in Provider ContractProvider Contract may inherit RAR relevance from: (1) Product master, (2) Item category, or (3) Existing contract data created before config change.
New service types still showing RAR flagCheck if Revenue Accounting Indicator is derived from BRIM integration settings (SD ↔ RAR integration customizing). Configuration at service type level alone may not control final flag.
RAR engine still receiving dataThere may be active integration via event-based revenue recognition or transfer control settings still marking items as relevant.
Configuration change not taking effectIf provider contracts already exist, system does not retroactively update them. Only new documents reflect config changes.
Where to Check1. Product Master → Revenue Accounting Indicator 2. Provider Contract Item Category Settings 3. SD → RAR Integration Customizing 4. Transfer of Revenue Line Items settings
Most Common Root CauseRAR relevance controlled at multiple levels — service type flag alone is not the final decision point.
How to Properly Exclude Service TypesEnsure all 3 layers are aligned: • Remove RAR relevance from Product Master • Adjust Item Category customizing • Verify SD-RAR integration settings • Test with NEW provider contract
If Existing Contracts Must Be FixedManual correction required (contract item adjustment) or enhancement via BAdI to suppress RAR transfer.
Technical Enhancement OptionImplement BAdI in BRIM/RAR integration layer to programmatically suppress transfer based on service type.
Testing RecommendationAlways test with completely new provider contract after configuration change to validate behavior.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: SAP GTS XML Filed mapping
entry.content:
Hello @Dipesh ,

 

  • If your XML follows SAP’s delivered schema → minimal configuration needed.

  • If XML comes from third-party compliance provider → mapping is mandatory.

  • Incorrect mapping results in:

    • Empty name fields

    • Missing address data

    • Upload errors in SLG1 logs

Step / QuestionExplanation & What To Do
Is field mapping required for XML upload?✅ Yes — if your XML structure does NOT exactly match the SAP GTS standard XML schema. If you use SAP-delivered schema format, no additional mapping is needed.
1️⃣ Check XML Format TypeConfirm whether you are using SAP standard SPL XML format or a third-party/custom XML format. If third-party → mapping required.
2️⃣ Identify Data Source TypeGo to: SPRO → SAP Global Trade Services → Compliance Management → Sanctioned Party List Screening → Configure Data Transfer → Define Data Source. Create or review your data source.
3️⃣ Define File Type (XML)In configuration, define File Type = XML and assign logical system / data provider. This tells GTS how to read the file.
4️⃣ Maintain Field MappingGo to: SPRO → SAP GTS → SPL Screening → Configure Data Transfer → Define Field Mapping for External Data. Here you map XML tags to GTS internal fields.
5️⃣ Map Header FieldsMap external XML tags like: • Name • Address • Country • Date of Birth • List Type to corresponding GTS fields (e.g., NAME1, CITY, COUNTRY, DOB, LIST_TYPE).
6️⃣ Maintain Structure MappingIf XML has nested nodes (e.g., multiple addresses or aliases), define structure mapping so GTS understands repeating segments.
7️⃣ Define Value Mapping (if needed)If external file uses different country codes or list codes, maintain value mapping via “Define Code Mapping”. Example: “USA” → “US”.
8️⃣ Test UploadUse transaction for SPL upload (e.g., /SAPSLL/SPL_UPLOAD depending on version). Load test file. Check application log (SLG1) for mapping errors.
9️⃣ Validate Imported RecordsAfter upload, check SPL master data display to confirm fields populated correctly.
🔟 Schedule Background Job (Optional)For recurring uploads, schedule background job with file directory monitoring.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Request for Creator and Approver Details of Treasury...
entry.content:
Hi @UNCOMMON_MEN ,

 

  • Creator is stored at deal header level.

  • Approver (Releaser) is stored in the release workflow / status log tables.

  • They are technically stored in different tables.

  • That is why no single classic table (like TJ08) shows both directly.

 

Requirement / ObservationExplanation & How to Retrieve Data
Need list of multiple Treasury deals (last month)Use standard deal list reports such as T-code FTR_EDIT (Display Multiple Transactions) or transaction manager reporting. Filter by transaction date or creation date.
Need “Who Created the Deal”Creator is stored in table TJ01 / VTBFHA (field ERNAM – Created By). This is standard and available in most deal list layouts.
Need “Who Approved / Released the Deal”Approval (release) is stored in the Release History tables (e.g., TRLS / VTB* release tables depending on configuration). It is not stored directly in TJ08.
TJ08 shows creator but not approverCorrect. TJ08 stores status history, but not always the username of the releaser in a directly visible column.
Is there a standard report showing both Creator and Approver together?There is no single classic T-code that directly shows both in one default layout. However, you can retrieve both via: • Transaction Manager → Environment → Status → Release History • Or create a custom ALV report using deal + release history tables.
Best Standard Option (No Custom Code)Use FTR_EDIT → Change Layout → Add fields: Created By (ERNAM). Then access Environment → Status → Release History for approver details.
If Mass Extraction Required (Audit Purpose)Best practice: Build a Query (SQVI / SQ01) or CDS view combining: • Deal Header table (VTBFHA) → Creator • Release History table (TRLS or VTB*_REL) → Approver (Released By field)
Fiori App AvailabilityNo standard Fiori app provides both Creator and Approver together in one delivered analytical report. Custom CDS-based analytical app is required if using Fiori.
Most Reliable Technical Source• Creator → VTBFHA-ERNAM • Approver → Release log tables (field AENAM / FREIGEGEBEN_VON depending on release strategy setup)
Recommended Approach for Audit ExtractDevelop one custom ALV / CDS report combining: Deal Number Company Code Product Type Created By Creation Date Released By Release Date

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: How to create a Segment in Fiori?
entry.content:
Hello @Kmbk227 ,

 

  • In S/4HANA, Segment is independent master data, not automatically derived from profit center.

  • You must create segment first before assigning it to profit center.

  • If you can create it in GUI (transaction FAGL_SEGM), Fiori is simply another UI layer — same backend object.

 

Step / RequirementWhat You Need To Do
 Understand Where Segment Is CreatedIn S/4HANA, a Segment is a master data object (table FAGL_SEGM). In Fiori, it is maintained via the Manage Global Hierarchies / Manage Segments app (depending on version).
Required Business RoleAssign business role: SAP_BR_GL_ACCOUNTANT or SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT (depending on system version). These roles provide access to financial master data maintenance.
Required Authorization ObjectEnsure authorization for object F_FAGL_SEG (Segment maintenance). Without this, the create button will not appear.
Required Fiori AppUse app: “Manage Global Hierarchies” (if using hierarchy-based segments) OR “Manage Segments” (if available in your version).
Required Catalog (If Custom Role)Add Business Catalog: SAP_FIN_BC_MASTER_DATA_PC (Profit Center Master Data) and SAP_FIN_BC_GL_MASTER_DATA. These contain segment maintenance apps.
Step 1 – Launch AppLog into Fiori Launchpad → Open Manage Global Hierarchies (or Manage Segments).
Step 2 – Create SegmentClick Create → Enter: • Segment ID (4–10 characters) • Description • Validity dates Save.
Step 3 – Assign to Hierarchy (If Required)If your system uses hierarchy-based reporting, assign the new segment into the relevant segment hierarchy structure.
 Step 4 – Transport (If Dev System)If working in DEV, ensure transport request is captured. Segment is customizing object.
Step 5 – Test in Profit Center CreationGo to Manage Profit Centers → Create or edit PC → Segment field should now display your newly created segment.
If App Not VisibleCheck: • Role assigned to user • Catalog assigned to role • Authorization object F_FAGL_SEG • ICF activation (if on-premise)

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: How to Prevent Unit Price from Being Split When Merging Documents?
entry.content:
When using the app Create Golden Tax Documents to merge multiple documents under certain materials and exchange rates, the system automatically generates only one line item, but the unit price is split across the merged items. Users may prefer to retain the original unit prices of the line items without splitting them. 

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to Prevent Unit Price from Being Split When Mergi...
entry.content:
This is standard behavior.

To retain the original unit prices of the line items without splitting them, the following methods can be considered:

Method 1: Adjust the Merge Group Field
1. Different codes can be assigned to the Merge Ref.Group field (e.g., 1, 2, 3) to create separate merge groups for the line items. 
2. Choose Merge Documents to process the merge. Multiple line items will be generated instead of a single one, and the unit prices will remain intact without being split.

Method 2: Use BAdI for Enhancement
The BAdI IDGTCN_OB_DATASET_PREP_AFT can be used to implement custom logic for document merging.

 

3721365- configuration for merging invoices based on material and unit price in nwbc golden tax output tax invoice platform https://me.sap.com/notes/3721365

what you are looking for is the Context Administration. In the Producer select the subtree you want to add the new version to. Then navigate to Tools > Context Administration > Edit Web Contexts 

In the drop down decide if you want to add the new version or set the version. Insert the new version in the Values field and hit OK. After the process is done save and publish the change to the Manager and be happy. 
One final information, all the elements in the subtree will get the version (groups, simulations, etc.)

entry.title: Re: Missing Legally Required Company Address Fields for E...
entry.content:
Hello @SAP4PC

Thank you for the follow up.

See my responses below.

Question: Which released A2X API is intended to be used to read Business Address Services by AddressID in Public Cloud?

Answer: I am not aware of any released API in Public Cloud that resolves a Company Code AddressID into full address fields on its own. The released address read capability is provided through BP address entities, where the key is BP + AddressID. See KBA 3715342 and https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/c17d3658e4870846e10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US for more details.

Question: rely on Business Partners with customer specific mapping logic? This clarification would be very helpful, as the missing seller address is currently a blocking issue for Belgian Peppol e invoicing compliance.

Answer: Use DRC with Peppol Exchange, where SAP manages the Peppol message generation and sender data as part of the solution. See https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-edition/sap-document-and-reporting-compliance-cloud-edition/registering-your-company-to-send-electronic-documents-in-peppol-network?locale=en-US for more details.

If you still use external Peppol, model the seller as a Legel Entity BP and retrieve the address via API_BUSINESS_PARTNER, though this means you are accepting that the Company Code to seller BP mapping relationship must be governed in your integration or master data design.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Beyond the Code: How Continuous Learning Shapes Sebastiano’s Path
entry.content:
What does learning look like when it becomes part of everyday life?

For Sebastiano, it starts early. On a quiet New York morning, before most of the city is fully awake. After an early run and a walk with his dog, he checks messages that arrived overnight. Somewhere between time zones, priorities, and projects, learning has become a steady rhythm in his day rather than a separate task, one he never sees as an obligation, but as momentum.

He has always been driven by the idea of setting goals and pushing beyond them. What gives those goals meaning, however, is people. Family, friends, colleagues, and communities play a central role in his life. He believes no achievement is truly individual; progress happens when personal ambition meets collective effort. That balance between striving forward and moving together keeps him motivated.

In his current role, Sebastiano works as a lead consultant at his company, moving fluidly between architectural decisions and product direction. His background in Computer Science provided him with a strong technical foundation, but his curiosity has always extended beyond pure development. Over time, that curiosity has drawn him closer to project management and finance, where he focuses on aligning technical solutions with business outcomes and long-term value.

Learning has been the thread connecting these two worlds, helping him deepen what he already knows while gradually expanding how he thinks about building systems and solutions.

That mindset took shape early in his career. Together with a couple of colleagues, Sebastiano developed a simple but meaningful ritual: meeting an hour before work to have breakfast and study for their next certification. It was a time marked by curiosity, shared motivation, and the energy of being young and eager to grow. Looking back, those mornings remain a happy memory and a reminder that learning is often at its best when it is shared.

His days are fast-paced and varied. He moves between multiple projects, supports colleagues, adds new features, resolves issues, and helps guide product direction. Amid constant context-switching, learning keeps him grounded. Twice a week, without exception, he sets aside time for research and self-study.

That commitment became even more natural with SAP Certification. As Sebastiano explains, “My work is highly SAP-centric, and staying up to date is essential. Learning allows me to achieve my career goals, add value to my company, and remain competitive.”

For him, SAP Learning brings clarity to a fast-moving field, offering a structured way to explore new topics, revisit fundamentals, and stay closely connected to innovation as it evolves.

However, learning is not always comfortable. Sebastiano admits new challenges can be mentally demanding. He approaches learning step by step, accepting uncertainty and asking for help when needed, from colleagues or newer tools such as AI. The reward comes when something finally clicks, bringing a deep sense of satisfaction. For him, learning is an important goal in itself.

One of the most defining outcomes of this journey has been becoming an SAP Champion. The recognition marked more than a professional milestone; it reshaped how Sebastiano sees himself. Through learning and knowledge sharing, he built credibility, formed connections, and strengthened trust in his abilities. SAP Learning gave him the foundation and confidence to share what he knows. While impostor syndrome never fully disappears, continuous learning and real-world application have steadily reduced its grip.

If he imagines the future, learning is still at the center. A park, a book, his dog nearby, and the chance to discover something new every day. For Sebastiano, learning is not a phase. It is a way forward: steady, shared, always evolving.

As he puts it, “Learning keeps my mind open and helps me move through change with confidence. It reminds me that growth doesn’t come from having all the answers, but from staying curious, asking for help, and continuing to move forward.”

Ready to begin your own SAP learning journey? Explore the SAP Learning site and take the next step toward growing your skills and shaping your future.

entry.title: Display of all business objects for maintenance orders or technical objects?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am looking for a way to retrieve all linked Business Objects or archived documents for a technical object or maintenance orders in a consolidated list.

Specifically, I am referring to the following Business Object types:

  • BUS2007 (Maintenance Order)
  • BUS0010 (Functional Location)

Using transaction OAOR, I can search by these object classes. However, the search is always limited to a single object instance.

Our requirement is to retrieve, for example:

  • maintenance documents
  • inspection reports
  • protocols
  • other documents created within maintenance orders

that were generated in the context of maintenance processing and linked either to the maintenance order or directly to the technical object.

Important clarification:
The solution should not be limited to a single maintenance order, but should instead search across all maintenance orders that are assigned to a specific technical object.

Example:
Functional Location “Pump Station X” is maintained regularly.
For each maintenance order, documents are created and attached via GOS / ArchiveLink.

Now, I would like to search for “Pump Station X” and receive a consolidated overview of all maintenance-related documents that were generated historically — regardless of which individual order they were created in.

My questions are:

  1. Is there any standard transaction or report that allows listing all linked documents for a technical object across all related maintenance orders?
  2. Can this be achieved via tables ?
  3. Is there an ArchiveLink or Content Server approach to generate such an object-based overview?
  4. Or would this require a custom development ?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: From Process to Architecture: 10 Use Cases of SAP Signavio & LeanIX Integration
entry.content:
SAP Signavio + SAP LeanIX

Why Process Transparency Without Architecture Context Is Not Enough

Within the SAP Integrated Toolchain, every solution plays a specific role:

  • SAP Signavio drives business process excellence

  • SAP LeanIX solutions enables enterprise architecture transparency
  • SAP Cloud ALMgoverns execution
  • Tricentis ensures quality for testing
  • SAP BTP enables extensibility

The integration between SAP Signavio and SAP LeanIX is not just a technical interface.
It is the strategic bridge between business process design and enterprise architecture governance.

In this article, we drill down into a Scenario of the Integrated Toolchain and explore why connecting these two worlds fundamentally changes how transformation programs are managed.

The Big Picture –-> Integrated Toolchain Overview:

The Signavio-LeanIX integration should be understood within the broader SAP transformation ecosystem.
It connects business design with architecture strategy as part of a fully integrated lifecycle.

We drill down on scenario number 3.

Map Processes to Architecture

By connecting process models in SAP Signavio with application landscapes in SAP LeanIX, organizations gain real-time visibility into:

  • Which applications support which processes

  • Where dependencies exist
  • Where redundancies slow down execution
  • Where legacy systems create bottlenecks

This enables full as-is transparency across business and IT layers.

Value:
This clarity enables rapid adaptation to change. When business processes evolve, the architectural impact is immediately visible ensuring continuous alignment between business strategy and IT architecture.

Analyze Impacts

Enable scenario-based decision-making using data-driven insights

Transformation rarely happens in isolation.

When an application is marked as:

  • End-of-life, Replacement candidate, Consolidation target the integration instantly reveals:

    • Which processes are affected

    • Which business units are impacted

    • Where operational risk might occur

Which capabilities are at risk

This makes scenario-based planning possible instead of reactive decision-making.

Value:
Understanding the potential impacts of architectural changes allows for informed, data-driven decisions.
It reduces transformation risk, strengthens executive alignment, and ensures strategic coherence across initiatives such as S/4HANA programs, cloud migrations, or M&A integrations.

 

First things first, the technical part: 

Here are two sources to help you te set up the integration. It is really straight forward and if you have the rights to do it it takes not long. 

SAP Signavio Integration from SAP LeanIX Documentation --> Documentation

Integrating SAP LeanIX and SAP Signavio --> Learning Journey 

Now when you are set up with the integration we can go into 10 use cases, why this integration is useful.

(The order of the use cases is random)

  1. End-to-End Transparency: Process-to-Application Mapping

    One of the most fundamental challenges in transformation programs is understanding:

    • Which applications support which processes?
    • Where are redundancies?

Where do critical processes depend on legacy systems?

By linking process models in Signavio to applications in LeanIX, organizations gain true end-to-end visibility across business and IT layers. This becomes a foundational capability for any transformation initiative.

When I click now on "ACME Marketplace" I see details coming from SAP LeanIX.

 

2. Transformation Impact Analysis

When an application in LeanIX is marked as:

  • End-of-life
  • To be replaced
  • Consolidation candidate

The integration immediately shows:

  • Which processes are affected
  • Which business units are impacted
  • What operational risk exists

This dramatically improves decision-making in S/4HANA migrations, cloud transitions, carve-outs, and IT modernization programs.

Deep dive here: Driving Business Transformation with the SAP Integ... - SAP Community from @JansiMurugesan 

 3. Business Capability-Based Planning

LeanIX excels at capability mapping.
Signavio excels at process modeling.

Together, they allow organizations to connect:

Strategy → Capabilities → Processes → Applications

This makes strategic investment planning significantly more data-driven and transparent.

Executives can clearly see:

  • Which capabilities are under-supported
  • Where process gaps exist
  • Where architectural complexity slows down innovation

Deep dive for this case: Bridging Strategy to Execution: How LeanIX, Signav... - SAP Community from @ramysalem 

 4. S/4HANA Transformation Governance

In large-scale transformations toward
SAP S/4HANA,
process and architecture transparency are critical.

The integration supports:

  • Identification of system-dependent process variants
  • Detection of redundant application usage
  • Governance of harmonized target processes
  • Alignment between business blueprint and IT landscape

It reduces transformation risk and increases implementation speed.

Deep dive for this use case: How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Val... - SAP Community from @ramysalem 

 5. Application Rationalization & Cost Optimization

Many organizations operate:

  • Multiple CRM systems
  • Several finance platforms
  • Shadow IT solutions

By mapping processes to applications, redundancy becomes visible.

This enables:

  • Reduction of overlapping systems
  • Consolidation roadmaps
  • Measurable cost savings
  • Reduced maintenance complexity

This use case directly contributes to IT budget optimization.

Deep dive: Application Rationalization: A Framework For Optimization

 6. Post-Merger Integration (M&A)

After acquisitions, organizations struggle with:

  • Duplicate applications
  • Parallel process landscapes
  • Inconsistent capability maturity

The integration allows organizations to:

  • Compare process landscapes across entities
  • Identify overlapping applications
  • Define harmonization roadmaps
  • Prioritize integration steps based on business impact

This significantly accelerates post-merger stabilization.

Deep dive: Post-merger integration: Removing risk, ensuring value

 7. Technical Debt Visibility

LeanIX provides lifecycle and technology risk insights.
Signavio shows business criticality of processes.

Together, they answer a key question:

Which business-critical processes depend on outdated or high-risk technologies?

This enables proactive modernization instead of reactive firefighting.

Deep dive: Technology Obsolescence Risk Management Part 2: The What

 8. Risk & Compliance Transparency

Regulated industries require traceability:

  • Which process uses which system?
  • Where is sensitive data processed?
  • How are controls implemented?

The integration supports ITGC, regulatory compliance, and audit-readiness by providing a single traceable chain from process to application. This is particularly valuable in Financial Services and Manufacturing environments.

Deep dive for this topic: SAP Signavio for Governance, Risk and Compliance (... - SAP Community from @dr_nganyadi 

 9. Business-IT Alignment in Executive Decision-Making

Often, CIOs speak architecture — COOs speak process.

The integration creates a shared language.

Executive dashboards can show:

  • Business impact of architectural decisions
  • Process complexity driven by system landscape
  • Transformation risk areas
  • Modernization prioritization

This alignment accelerates strategic decisions.

Deep dive for this use case: How Enterprise Architects Can Unlock Strategic Val... - SAP Community from @ramysalem 

 10. Foundation for an Integrated Transformation Toolchain

The integration of Signavio and LeanIX is not an isolated scenario.

It is the foundation for a broader transformation toolchain including:

  • Process Design (Signavio)
  • Enterprise Architecture (LeanIX)
  • Implementation Governance
  • Test & Quality Automation

This enables a connected journey from:

Strategy → Process → Architecture → Execution → Validation

Deep dive for this case: Why the SAP Integrated Toolchain Is the Future of ... - SAP Community from @ramysalem 

Conclusion

Sustainable transformation requires more than isolated transparency. Process excellence and architecture governance must operate on a shared foundation.

The integration of SAP Signavio and SAP LeanIX establishes this foundation by connecting business process design with enterprise architecture context. It enables organizations to understand not only how processes are executed, but also which systems support them, where dependencies exist, and what impact architectural decisions will have on business operations. This shift from fragmented visibility to connected insight fundamentally improves transformation governance. Scenario planning becomes more precise, modernization efforts more targeted, and executive decisions more evidence-based.

In complex transformation environments whether S/4HANA programs, cloud migrations, application rationalization, or M&A integration this integration supports structured, risk-aware decision-making.

It does not simplify transformation.
It makes it manageable.

 

Thank you for your contribution @annika1998 & @marczintel

entry.title: LGM Knowledge Snippet: Automated (BN4L-ID based) vs Manual Subcontracting
entry.content:
When you work with subcontracting in SAP Logistics Management, you might wonder: how does the system know whether to wait for an automated carrier response or require manual confirmation/rejection? The answer lies in a simple but crucial piece of business partner data: the BN4L ID.

What Is BN4L?

BN4L stands for Business Network for Logistics — SAP’s cloud-based collaboration platform that connects shippers with carriers. Through BN4L, SAP Logistics Management can send subcontracting requests to carriers and receive their responses — all automatically, without any manual actions.

However, not every carrier is connected to BN4L. Some may use traditional communication channels, such as phone or email, to confirm their transportation orders. SAP Logistics Management must determine which carriers can communicate via BN4L and which require manual handling.

The BN4L ID: Your Carrier’s Digital Fingerprint

The key factor in deciding whether a carrier is connected to BN4L is the BN4L ID (also known as the LBN ID), maintained as an external identifier on the carrier’s business partner data. When a carrier is onboarded to SAP Business Network for Logistics, they receive this unique identifier, which enables electronic communication.

In SAP S/4HANA, this ID is maintained in the business partner master data under the Identification section with the identification type LBN001 (SAP Business Network for Logistics ID).

Subcontracting In SAP Logistics Management

So, in SAP Logistics Management, the BN4L ID is used for subcontracting mode determination and serves as the primary identification for automated communication with carriers via BN4L. To enable this communication, the BN4L integration must be properly configured. This understanding sets the stage for a detailed look at the subcontracting process in LGM.

Subcontracting Mode Determination

The process starts when the Send to Carrier action is triggered for a consignment. SAP Logistics Management checks the carrier’s business partner identification data for the BN4L ID to determine how confirmation should be handled:

  • If the carrier has a BN4L ID. The system expects an electronic confirmation via SAP Business Network for Logistics, and no manual action is required.
  • If the carrier does not have a BN4L ID. The system expects manual confirmation/rejection after contacting the carrier outside the system.

This BN4L-based mechanism is particularly relevant in the Direct LTL shipping process, where the transportation order is sent for subcontracting after the tendering process

Identifying BN4L Carriers On The UI

In the Manage Consignments app, you can add the Carrier column to your list report view. This indicator shows whether the assigned carrier has a maintained BN4L ID, helping you quickly identify which consignments will receive automated responses and which require manual handling.

Subcontracting Process Flow

  • For Non-BN4L Carriers (Manual Process)

The consignment Carrier Confirmation Status is set to Confirmation Pending, and the system marks the consignment with Action Required (reason: Open Task) to highlight this manual step.

At the end of the subcontracting process, a manual action must be triggered to update the consignment Carrier Confirmation Status to Confirmed or Rejected.

  • For BN4L Carriers (Automated Process)

The Carrier Confirmation Status updates to Confirmation Pending, with additional information indicating that the system is waiting for a response from the carrier via SAP Business Network for Logistics.

The consignment Carrier Confirmation Status automatically updates to Confirmed or Rejected based on the carrier response.

Setting Up BN4L Integration In SAP Logistics Management

To enable BN4L subcontracting, the integration must be configured using the following steps:

  • Maintain the BN4L ID in S/4HANA. Add an identification of type LBN001 to the carrier’s Business Partner master data in SAP S/4HANA. The LBN ID must be retrieved from the SAP Business Network for Logistics and also set on the master data record.
  • Replicate the Business Partners to SAP Logistics Management. Once the carrier data is updated, replicate the Business Partner to SAP Logistics Management using the Business Partner Replication process from the connected S/4 system. Replication can run automatically via a scheduled job or ad-hoc.
  • Configure the BN4L Connection. Set up the technical connection to BN4L by maintaining the communication system, creating the communication arrangement, and configuring the destination with the correct authentication details. Follow the BN4L Integration Setup guide to ensure successful communication between SAP Logistics Management and BN4L.

Wrapping Up

In SAP Logistics Management, the subcontracting confirmation mode is determined by verifying whether a BN4L ID is assigned to the carrier — its presence enables automated electronic confirmations, while its absence requires manual confirmation/rejection.

This approach makes managing subcontracting confirmations simple and clear:

  • Single source of truth. The BN4L ID on the carrier master data shows whether a carrier can use BN4L. You don’t need extra flags or settings.
  • Automatic updates. When a carrier connects to BN4L and you replicate their data, their consignments automatically switch to electronic confirmation.
  • Works with all carriers. BN4L carriers get automated confirmations, while carriers not connected to BN4L are handled manually. You can manage both types in the same system without issues.

entry.title: Navigating Your Move to BI 2025 and Beyond with SAP’s Eric Fenollosa & Gregory Botticchio
entry.content:
Eric Fenollosa and Gregory Botticchio joined Allan Pym of APOS recently to review the BusinessObjects roadmap and product direction. Many of the updates they discussed were very recent, including the availability of Lumira 2025, which became available only hours before the start of the webinar.

To start, Allan polled the audience about the status of BusinessObjects within their respective organizations and discovered:

  • 79% of the audience was on BI 4.3; 15% were on BI 4.2 or earlier; and only a small minority had already made the move to BI 2025.
  • 30% would be migrating in the next 6 to 12 months; 28% in the next 6 months; 15% were currently migrating; and 26% either had no migration plans or are more than a year away from migrating.
  • A large majority (79%) would be migrating to BI 2025, while 9% would be migrating directly to BI 2027; 7% will make the move to BI 4.3.

Allan then introduced Eric and Gregory.

Eric’s portion of the presentation was dedicated to maintenance timelines and the product roadmap, while Gregory focused on demonstrating the latest features for Web Intelligence.

The maintenance timeline slide is by now familiar to most of us, but it is important to note that BI 4.3 maintenance ends at the end of this year, with security fixes continuing through 2027. Thus, the end of 2027 will mark the end of the BI 4.x versions of BusinessObjects, though customers may choose to continue with Customer Specific Maintenance

New major releases will occur every 2 years – BI 2025, BI 2027, BI 2029, and so on. Each major release will enjoy 3 years of maintenance plus 1 year of security fixes.

Eric emphasized that, although the roadmap only goes as far as BI 2029, it doesn’t mean that 2029 is the end of the road for BusinessObjects. SAP sees no reason to discontinue BusinessObjects in the foreseeable future.

For more information, this slide presents the following links:

  • SAP Maintenance Strategy
  • SAP Analytics Statement of Direction
  • SAP BusinessObjects BI Maintenance Strategy Document

Breaking News: as of the day of this webinar (February 25, 2026), Lumira 2025 is available! Eric tells us that reorganizations of development teams under BusinessObjects leadership will allow Lumira releases to keep pace with platform releases in the future.

The BusinessObjects portfolio slide shows the rationalization strategy that will allow SAP to focus on delivering innovations for the most important components of the solution. Accordingly, the following components are not available in BI 2025:

  • Lumira Discovery
  • Crystal Reports for Enterprise
  • Analysis for OLAP
  • Live Office
  • Universe Design Tool

Innovations in Web Intelligence and Crystal Reports will allow users to meet the use case requirements for which the deprecated components were formerly used.

The biggest hurdle to BI 2025 adoption is the move from UNV to UNX universes. This change must be accomplished before migration to BI 2025.

SAP Analysis for Office and SAP Lumira Designer will be maintained and supported, but no new features will be delivered.

Eric then turned to the Product direction slide.

Eric focused on the BI 2027 column on this slide, talking about BI Launchpad modernization, Governance, and AI readiness for BI.

The new BI classification feature will allow organizations to attach classifications to reports for added security and governance.

The pace of change in the world of AI is so rapid that it makes little sense to include specific AI versions in an on-premise platform such as BusinessObjects. Rather than develop actual AI functionality within BI 2025/2027/2029, SAP will focus on ensuring BusinessObjects’ robust set of APIs is AI ready. AI readiness will benefit organizations within the SAP partner ecosphere, many of which are already developing AI offerings for your consideration.

Eric then turned to Crystal Reports.

Crystal Reports development for BI 2027 is focused on helping customers move from the Enterprise version to Crystal Reports 202x, as the Enterprise version is no longer supported. The Crystal Reports 2027 SDK will increase power users’ productivity, and security will be enhanced.

At this point, Gregory took over the presentation to discuss innovations in Web Intelligence.

Gregory also focused on what is coming in BI 2027, including enhancements to:

  • Visualizations, interactivities and Dashboarding
  • Shared analysis and assets off-line distribution
  • Report creation workflows
  • AI readiness for AI

As Eric noted earlier, innovations in core components such as Web Intelligence will largely replace the functionality of deprecated components.

Gregory then turned to a demonstration of upcoming BI 2027 Web Intelligence functionality.

Gregory showed us new chart interactivity that will be available in BI 2027, where users can use new widgets to display charts flexibly, drill down into the data, view pop-ups, and filter and export data.

To see the demo, watch the webinar on demand here.

As Eric noted earlier in the presentation, the biggest hurdle to BI 2025 adoption is the move from UNV to UNX universes. Allan provided a brief description of the APOS Web Intelligence Migrator and how it can simplify and expedite this process.

The APOS Web Intelligence Migrator is one example of how APOS automation can make your move to BI 2025 easier and less stressful.

A question-and-answer session followed. Watch the webinar on demand here for specifics.

entry.title: Unable to edit validity periods for tax code
entry.content:
We  tried to edit the validity period in configuration activity Configuration Activity 101016 "Define Tax Codes for Sales and Purchases" (Finance > Statutory Reporting > Tax).

But we are unable to edit the validity periods. Kindly review and advise. 

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unable to edit validity periods for tax code
entry.content:
See kba 3056410 - 'Deactivate Line' and 'Delete Last Rate Change' of tax codes in Configuration Activity 101016

Resolution

'Deactivate Line' function is only available when the tax code has one validity period.

Therefore where there is multiple validity Periods first use function  'Delete Last Rate Change' undo changes made for a tax code with multiple conditions or multiple validity periods. This option reverts the tax rates for this tax code to the previous saved settings. 

After same. You can use 'Deactivate Line' Function.

entry.title: AI-Assisted Commodity Work Center: For Smarter Commodity Trading
entry.content:
Commodity trading has never been more complex or more time sensitive. Global energy and agriculture markets move at extraordinary speed, shaped by volatile prices, regulatory shifts, logistics constraints, and constant streams of data. In this environment, traders don’t just need more information—they need intelligent support that helps them act faster and with confidence. 

That’s where AI Assisted Commodity Work Center (ACW) comes in. 

ACW is purpose-built to empower commodity traders with intelligent automation, real-time interaction, and seamless collaboration, all designed to match the pace and complexity of modern trading operations. 

 

Automate What Matters Most 

Traders should spend their time making decisions, not formatting documents or chasing repetitive tasks. 

ACW introduces AI agents that streamline and accelerate document creation directly through BTP to SAP S/4HANA by processing trade summaries written in natural language using Large Language Models. From trade summaries to the creation of draft commodity deals and contracts, ACW automates data capture tasks that traditionally slow traders down. 

By eliminating manual steps and reducing rework: 

  • Uses natural language to capture and process trade summaries from various sources—such as chats, emails, meeting transcripts, and more—so traders don’t need to follow specific trade summary formats 
  • Examples: 
    • Purchase 3 Ships (28,000 barrels 60F / ship) ULSD Diesel from Oil Company, one lifting per month for JFM 2026 (3 total lifts), priced at the Monthly Average Chicago OPIS Index plus $0.05. FOB Chicago Ship. 
    • Purchase Golden Harvest Inc 1000 MT Soybeans Grade A CIF Baton Rouge ZSK2026 Jan - March by ship 
  • Traders reclaim valuable time during peak trading hours 
  • Errors caused by manual data capture are significantly reduced 
  • Operational efficiency improves without disrupting existing systems 

The result? Traders stay focused on strategy and execution, while ACW handles the administrative elements of deal capture.  

 

Capture deals with confidence 

In commodity markets, timing is everything. Delayed decisions can mean missed opportunities, or increased risk. 

ACW delivers real-time AI driven data capture that allows traders to instantly convert trade summaries into actionable documents. Instead of switching between systems or waiting on downstream processes, traders can move seamlessly from insight to execution. 

With ACW: 

  • Critical information is surfaced and validated when it’s needed most 
  • Trade-related data is contextualized, not just displayed 
  • Data capture becomes faster, more accurate, and more consistent across multiple deal types 

Progress Bar

With error indicator Indicating warnings to be reviewed by the user Ready to create the document 

This means that traders can replace their traditional handwritten deal sheets/trade books by writing the same trade summary into ACW and have the trade created in the system. 

 

Scale with intelligence 

As organizations grow, so do their trading teams. But scaling expertise has traditionally been difficult; new traders face steep learning curves, complex systems, and fragmented workflows. ACW changes that dynamic. 

With an intuitive, trader-centric user interface, ACW enables rapid deal capture for every trader, regardless of experience level. The platform is designed to feel natural, reducing onboarding time and helping teams become productive faster. 

This means: 

  • Faster ramp-up for new traders 
  • More consistent execution across teams 
  • Institutional knowledge embedded into everyday workflows 

Message Bar

Easy-to-understand warning and error messages helping traders to identify what needs to be done. 

 

Built for the reality of commodity markets 

ACW is not a generic AI tool, it’s designed specifically for commodity trading organizations, where precision, speed, and compliance matter. By integrating directly into existing enterprise environments and aligning with real trader workflows, ACW enhances how teams work without forcing them to work differently. 

 

Conclusion - The Future of Trading Starts Here 

ACW represents a new standard for commodity trading operations—one where AI works alongside traders, not instead of them. By automating what matters most, delivering real-time AI driven data capture, and scaling intelligence across teams, ACW enables traders to operate with greater speed, clarity, and confidence. 

In markets where every decision counts, ACW is the AI Work Center that keeps traders ahead. 

SAP Help Documentation 

  • Admin Guide: Overview | SAP Help Portal
